title: Tyre, Lebanon
content:
Tyre (;;, Greek "Tyros", Τύρος) is one of the oldest continually inhabited cities in the world, though in medieval times for some centuries by just a tiny population. It was one of the earliest Phoenicia n metropolis es and the legend ary birthplace of Europa, her brothers Cadmus and Phoenix, as well as Carthage's founder Dido (Elissa). The city has many ancient sites, including the Tyre Hippodrome, and was added as a whole to UNESCO's list of World Heritage Site s in 1984. The historian Ernest Renan noted that "One can call Tyre a city of ruins, built out of ruins".

Today Tyre is the fourth largest city in Lebanon after Beirut, Tripoli, and Sidon. It is the capital of the Tyre District in the South Governorate. There were approximately 200,000 inhabitants in the Tyre urban area in 2016, including many refugee s, as the city hosts three of the twelve Palestinian refugee camps in Lebanon: Burj El Shimali, El Buss, and Rashidieh. Territory.

<mapframe latitude="33.27" longitude="35.214" zoom="11" width="220" height="235" align="right" / Tyre juts out from the coast of the <a href="Mediterranean%20Sea" Mediterranean Sea</a and is located about south of <a href="Beirut" Beirut</a.

It originally consisted of two distinct urban centres: Tyre itself, which was on an island just offshore, and the associated settlement of <a href="Ushu" Ushu</a on the adjacent mainland, later called Palaetyrus, meaning "Old Tyre" in <a href="Ancient%20Greek" Ancient Greek</a.

Throughout history from <a href="Prehistory" prehistoric times</a onwards, all settlements in the Tyre area profited from the abundance of <a href="fresh%20water" fresh water</a supplies, especially from the nearby <a href="Spring%20%28hydrology%29" springs</a of Rashidieh and <a href="Ras%20al-Ain%2C%20Lebanon" Ras Al Ain</a in the South.

In addition there are the springs of <a href="Al%20Bagbog" Al Bagbog</a and <a href="Ain%20Ebreen" Ain Ebreen</a in the North as well as the <a href="Litani%20River" Litani River</a, also known as Alqasymieh. The present city of Tyre covers a large part of the original island and has expanded onto and covers most of the causeway built by <a href="Alexander%20the%20Great" Alexander the Great</a in 332 BCE.

This <a href="isthmus" isthmus</a increased greatly in width over the centuries because of extensive silt depositions on either side. The part of the original island not covered by the modern city of Tyre is mostly of an archaeological site showcasing remains of the city from ancient times.

Four municipalities contribute to Tyre city's 16.7 km2 built-up area, though none are included in their entirety: Sour municipality contains the heart of the city, excluding the Natural and Coastal Reserve; Burj El Shimali to the East without unpopulated agricultural lands; <a href="Abbasiyet%20Sour" Abbasiyet Sour</a to the North without agricultural lands and a dislocated village; and <a href="Ain%20Baal" Ain Baal</a to the South-East, also without agricultural lands and dislocated villages.

Tyre's urban area lies on a fertile coastal plain, which explains the fact that as of 2017 about 44% of its territory was used for intra-urban agriculture, while built-up land constituted over 40%. In terms of <a href="geomorphology" geomorphology</a and <a href="seismicity" seismicity</a, Tyre is close to the <a href="Roum%20Fault" Roum Fault</a and the <a href="Yammoune" Yammoune</a h <a href="Fault%20%28geology%29" Fault</a.

Though it has suffered a number of devastating earthquakes over the <a href="Millennium" millennia</a, the threat level is considered to be low in most places and moderate in a few others. However, a <a href="tsunami" tsunami</a following an earthquake and subsequent <a href="landslide" landslide</a s and floods pose major natural risks to the Tyrian population.

Vast reserves of natural gas are estimated to lie beneath Lebanese waters, much of it off Tyre's coast, but <a href="Exploitation%20of%20natural%20resources" exploitation</a has been delayed by <a href="Territorial%20dispute" border disputes</a with Israel. Etymology. Early names of Tyre include <a href="Akkadian%20language" Akkadian</a "Ṣurru", <a href="Phoenician%20languages" Phoenician</a "Ṣūr" (), and <a href="Hebrew%20language" Hebrew</a "Tzór" ().

In <a href="Semitic%20languages" Semitic languages</a, the name of the city means "rock" after the rocky formation on which the town was originally built. The predominant form in <a href="Classical%20Greek" Classical Greek</a was "Týros" (Τύρος), which was first seen in the works of <a href="Herodotus" Herodotus</a but may have been adopted considerably earlier.

It gave rise to <a href="Latin" Latin</a "Tyrus", which entered <a href="English%20language" English</a during the <a href="Middle%20English" Middle English</a period as "Tyre". The <a href="demonym" demonym</a for Tyre is "Tyrian", and the inhabitants are "Tyrians". Climate.

Tyre has a <a href="Mediterranean%20climate" Hot-summer mediterranean climate</a (classified as "Csa" under the <a href="K%C3%B6ppen%20climate%20classification" Köppen climate classification</a), characterized by six months of drought from May to October. On average, it has 300 days of <a href="sun" sun</a a year and a yearly temperature of 20.8<a href="Celsius" °C</a. The average maximum temperature reaches its highest at 30.8 °C in August and the average minimum temperature its lowest at 10 °C in January.

On average, the mean annual <a href="precipitation" precipitation</a reaches up to 645 mm. The temperature of the <a href="Seawater" sea water</a reaches a minimum of 17 °C in February and a maximum of 32 °C in August. At a depth of 70 m it is constantly at 17–18 °C.

Meanwhile, <a href="Sea%20level%20rise" rising sea levels</a due to <a href="global%20warming" global warming</a threaten <a href="coastal%20erosion" coastal erosion</a to Tyre's peninsula and bay areas. History. Founding Millennium (2750–1700 BCE). The Roman historian <a href="Justin%20%28historian%29" Justin</a wrote that the original founders arrived from the nearby Northern city of <a href="Sidon" Sidon</a / Saida in the quest to establish a new harbour.
